coredata.dc:description: Converting CO2 to value-added syngas is an effective approach to relieve environmental problems and energy crisis simultaneously. However, it's still a challenge to establish long-wavelength irradiation technology in association with tuning H2: CO proportion. Therefore, carbon vacancy modified carbon quantum dots were integrated with g-C3N4 to form VCQDs/C3N4 heterojunctions, which exhibited superior performance for CO2 photoreduction in the presence of sacrificial agent and electron mediator under visible light irradiation. This was attributed to the strong interaction between g-C3N4 and VCQDs and carbon vacancy in VCQDs, which can facilitate the migration of photo-generated electrons and broaden the absorbance range of heterojunctions. Besides, the H2: CO ratio of produced syngas can be tuned by adjusting the VCQD dosage in VCQDs/C3N4, and a 3 : 1 ratio is obtained over VCQD(25 μL)/C3N4 in conjunction with Co(bpy)3Cl2·6H2O cocatalyst. This is significant for industrial application of syngas and for relieving the greenhouse effect brought by CO2.

head.ce:title: Integrating carbon vacancy modified carbon quantum dots with carbon nitride for efficient photocatalytic CO2 reduction to syngas with tunable hydrogen to carbon monoxide ratio

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Converting CO2 to value-added syngas is an effective approach to relieve environmental problems and energy crisis simultaneously. However, it's still a challenge to establish long-wavelength irradiation technology in association with tuning H2: CO proportion. Therefore, carbon vacancy modified carbon quantum dots were integrated with g-C3N4 to form VCQDs/C3N4 heterojunctions, which exhibited superior performance for CO2 photoreduction in the presence of sacrificial agent and electron mediator under visible light irradiation. This was attributed to the strong interaction between g-C3N4 and VCQDs and carbon vacancy in VCQDs, which can facilitate the migration of photo-generated electrons and broaden the absorbance range of heterojunctions. Besides, the H2: CO ratio of produced syngas can be tuned by adjusting the VCQD dosage in VCQDs/C3N4, and a 3 : 1 ratio is obtained over VCQD(25 μL)/C3N4 in conjunction with Co(bpy)3Cl2·6H2O cocatalyst. This is significant for industrial application of syngas and for relieving the greenhouse effect brought by CO2.

The as-synthesized VCQDs/g-C3N4 heterojunctions exhibit much better visible-light activity for the photoreduction of CO2 to syngas with tunable hydrogen to carbon monoxide ratio than g-C3N4. This is because there is a strong interfacial interaction between g-C3N4 matrix and VCQDs; and the carbon vacancy in VCQDs can facilitate the migration of photo-generated electrons from carbon nitride to VCQD and broaden the absorbance range of the heterojunctions. Moreover, the H2: CO ratio of the as-produced syngas over VCQDs(25 μL)/C3N4 can be tuned by altering the loading amount of VCQDs.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: With the rapid development of industry in human society, a large amount of CO2 was emitted into the atmosphere in recent decades. The greenhouse effect accompanying CO2 emission has caused climate change and destroyed the ecological balance of the earth [1–4]. Converting CO2 into valuable chemicals by solar energy is a “One Stone Two Birds” solution, addressing resource depletion and climate anomalies. Among the products of the photocatalytic reduction of CO2, the mixture of H2 and CO (syngas) is of particular significance, since it can be used in large-scale industrial processes to synthesize methane [5], hydrocarbons [6–8], and alcohol [9,10] through a Fischer–Tropsch process. However, most of currently available photocatalysts for the photocatalytic reduction of CO2 can be activated only by ultraviolet (UV) which occupies about only 5% of the solar spectrum. Therefore, it is a challenge to explore novel visible-light-activated photocatalysts for the photoreduction of CO2 in industry. Another challenge in this respect is how to control the proportion of CO and H2 in syngas, because the use of the as-produced syngas highly depends on its composition.

In recent years, graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4), a metal-free polymer semiconductor, was widely used in water splitting for hydrogen evolution [11], photocatalytic elimination of organic pollutants [12] and photocatalytic reduction of CO2 [13,14], due to its low cost, nontoxicity, good visible-light responsiveness and excellent thermal and chemical stability. However, pure g-C3N4 photocatalysts still suffer from the high recombination probability of photogenerated charges and poor visible light utilization [15,16]. This drawback of g-C3N4 photocatalyst could be overcome by constructing heterojunctions such as Bi3O4Cl/g-C3N4 [17], NiO/g-C3N4 [18], Bi2Se3/g-C3N4 [19], NiAl-LDH/C3N4 [20], and carbon quantum dots/g-C3N4 [21] to promote the separation of photogenerated carriers. Among them, carbon quantum dots/g-C3N4 heterojunction is widely used in water splitting [22] and photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants [23,24], thanks to the excellent performance of carbon quantum dots such as low cost, chemical stability, unique electron transfer and electron storage properties. To our surprise, few are currently available about the photocatalytic reduction of CO2 by carbon quantum dots/g-C3N4 yielding syngas.

Herein, we combine g-C3N4 with carbon quantum dots (denoted as CQDs) containing carbon vacancy (denoted as VCQDs) to obtain VCQDs/C3N4 heterojunctions for CO2 photoreduction affording syngas. This article reports the construction and characterization of the VCQDs/C3N4 heterojunctions as well as the evaluation of their charge-carrier separation efficiency and catalytic activity for the photoreduction of CO2 producing syngas under visible light irradiation. It also deals with the feasibility to tune the H2: CO ratio of the as-produced syngas by adjusting the amount of VCQDs loaded on the surface of g-C3N4. The present approach, hopefully, could help to shed light on the development of highly efficient photocatalysts for the production of syngas with adjustable H2: CO ratio via CO2 photoreduction.

Urea, citric acid (CA), cobalt chloride hexahydrate (CoCl2·6H2O), nickel chloride hexahydrate (NiCl2·6H2O), potassium chloride (KCl), sodium dihydrogen phosphate (NaH2PO4), triethanolamine (TEA), 2,2′-dipyridyl and acetonitrile (CH3CN) were purchased from Kemiou Chemical Reagent Company (Tianjin, China). Tris (2,2-bipyridyl) ruthenium (II) chloride hexahydrate (Ru(bpy)3Cl2·6H2O) was purchased from Bide Pharmatech Company Limited (Shanghai, China). All chemicals were used as-received.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of VCQDs and CQDs solutions
ce:section.ce:para: VCQDs solution was prepared by a simple hydrothermal method described elsewhere [25]. As shown in Scheme 1 , citric acid (15 g) and urea (5 g) were dissolved in 75 mL of deionized water and ultrasonicated for 10 min to form a transparent solution. The mixed solution was transferred into a 100-mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 5 h to obtain a reddish-brown colloidal solution. During the reaction, ammonia gas with reducibility is released upon the addition of urea, which is beneficial to the formation of carbon vacancy. Upon completion of reaction, the colloidal solution was centrifuged at 3000 rev/min for 20 min; and the supernatant containing VCQDs was collected. Similarly, CQDs solution was prepared in the same manners while no urea was added during heating citric acid solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of VCQDs(xμL)/C3N4 and CQDs(25 μL)/C3N4 catalysts
ce:section.ce:para: 10 g of urea as the precursor of g-C3N4 was dissolved into 20 mL of deionized water and ultrasonicated for 10 min to obtain a transparent solution. A certain volume of VCQDs solution (0 μL, 10 μL, 25 μL, 50 μL, 75 μL) was then added into the solution under 10 min of stirring, followed by heating at 60 °C in an oven to remove water and afford solid powder product. The as-obtained solid powder was put into an alumina crucible and annealed at 550 °C for 3 h in air to yield VCQDs(xμL)/C3N4 catalyst (x represents the volume of the added VCQDs solution). CQDs(25 μL)/C3N4 was synthesized in the same manners by replacing VCQDs solution with CQDs solution and used for comparative studies.

The crystal structure of the as-synthesized catalysts was identified with an X-ray diffractometer (XRD Bruker D8 Advance; Cu Kα radiation, 40 kV, 40 mA). Their morphologies were observed with a transmission electron microscope (TEM; JEM-F200, accelerating voltage: 200 kV). The chemical states of the major elements of the as-synthesized catalysts were analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS; Thermo Fisher Scientific Escalab 250Xi; Al Kα source), and the binding energy of contaminated carbon (C1s: 284.80 eV) was used as a reference. Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR; Bruker ESR A300-10/12 spectrometer) signals were recorded at room temperature (298 K) to detect the existence of carbon vacancy. A VERTEX 70 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FT-IR) was performed over the wavelength range of 4000–400 cm−1 to analyze the chemical feature of the as-synthesized catalysts. The absorbance behaviors of the CQDs solution and VCQDs solution were studied with an ultraviolet–visible light (UV–Vis; Persee TU-1810) spectrophotometer. The ultraviolet visible diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) were measured with an UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Persee TU-1901) equipped with an integrating sphere attachment. The photoluminescence spectra (PL) were scanned with a fluorescence spectrophotometer (F7000, Japan) under an excitation wavelength of 325 nm. The time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) spectra were measured with an FLS980 spectrophotometer (Edinburgh Instruments). An accelerated surface area and porosimetry system (Quadrasorb SI-4) was used to measure the specific surface area (BET) and pore size distribution of the as-prepared catalysts at 77 K.

The photocatalytic reduction of CO2 was conducted with a quartz glass reactor (200-mL) equipped with a Beijing Perfectlight 300 W xenon lamp (PLS-SXE300) as the visible light source (Fig. S1). In a typical experiment, 50 mg of the catalyst and a certain amount of the mixture of MCl2·6H2O and bpy or as-synthesized [M(bpy)3]Cl2·6H2O (M = Co, Ni and Ru; bpy = 2,2′-bipyridine) were dispersed in 7 mL of the mixed solution of acetonitrile (4 mL), H2O (2 mL), and triethanolamine (TEOA; 1 mL). Here TEOA serves as a sacrificial agent and [M(bpy)3]2+ acts as an electron mediator. [M(bpy)3]2+ like [Co(bpy)3]2+ was added into the reaction system in three modes to explore the optimal reaction condition. In mode A, CoCl2·6H2O and bpy with the molar ratio of 1 : 3 are added into the reaction system simultaneously; in mode B, CoCl2·6H2O and bpy with equal mass (20 mg) are added into the reaction system simultaneously; and in mode C, 20 mg of the as-synthesized [Co(bpy)3]Cl2·6H2O is added into the reaction system. The details about the synthesis method of [Co(bpy)3]Cl2·6H2O and [Ni(bpy)3]Cl2·6H2O are available in supplementary material; and [Ru(bpy)3]Cl2·6H2O is purchased from Bide Pharmatech Company Limited.

In a typical testing process, the mixed solution of acetonitrile (4 mL), H2O (2 mL), and TEOA (1 mL) as well as a certain amount of electron mediator was added into the reactor and degassed to eliminate air while highly pure CO2 gas (99.999%) was input. The CO2 pressure in the reactor is about 0.1 MPa. The reactor was then illuminated with a 300-W Xe lamp (PLS-SXE300, Beijing Perfectlight) equipped with a 420-nm cut-off filter under an visible light intensity of 23 mW/cm2 (λ = 420 nm). During the illumination process, the reactor was surrounded with circulating water to keep a reaction temperature of 25 °C. The experiments were performed in intermittent mode and the generated gaseous products were injected into gas chromatography (GC; Techcomp GC 7900), which was equipped with a flame ionization detector (FID) and a thermal conductivity detector (TCD), to be analyzed per hour. The as-generated CO and H2 were detected by FID with a methanization furnace and TCD, respectively. The detection was repeated three times at each time point. In the process of photocatalysis, the reaction system was stirred by magnetic agitator and the reaction time was 4 h.

The transient photocurrent response was measured with an electrochemical workstation (CHI630E, China) equipped with a standard three-electrode photoelectrochemical cell. The working electrode was prepared as follows: 20 mg of the to-be-tested catalyst was dispersed in 0.5 mL of ultrapure water, followed by the addition of 50 μL of Nafion solution (5 wt%) to form a homogeneous slurry. The as-obtained slurry was uniformly coated onto the indium-tin-oxide (2.0 cm × 1.5 cm) glass to obtain the working electrode. The Ag/AgCl electrode and a Pt filament were used as the reference electrode and counter electrode, respectively. Electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) were measured with a Zennium pro electrochemical workstation (ZAHNER, Germany) in the frequency range of 100 kHz–0.1 Hz under visible light illumination, with which the solution of 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3-/[Fe(CN)6]4- and 0.5 M KCl was used as the electrode solution. The Mott–Schottky plots were obtained in the potential range of −1.5 V–0.5 V (potential step: 50 mV) at frequencies of 2.0 kHz and 1.5 kHz at room temperature in the dark. The aqueous solution of 0.1 M Na2SO4 was used as the electrolyte. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) was carried out on a CHI630E instrument in the solution containing 20 mL MeCN, 10 mL H2O and 2 mL TEOA, combined with a glassy carbon working electrode, a platinum-wire counter electrode and a Ag/AgCl reference electrode. The electrocatalytic behavior were tested from −0.2 V to - 1.8 V at 100 mV•s−1.

The nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms were measured to analyze the specific surface area and pore size distribution of the samples. All of the as-prepared catalysts exhibit type IV isotherms with H3 hysteresis loop, which reveals that they contain mesopores (Fig. S2). The presence of abundant micropores could facilitate the transfer of reactants and charge carriers and promote the photocatalytic activity of catalyst [27,28]. The specific surface area of the as-prepared samples calculated by the BET mode is displayed in Table 1. The surface area of pure C3N4 is 71 m2/g, and that of the as-synthesized catalysts decreases gradually from 65 m2/g (VCQDs(10 μL)/C3N4) to 52 m2/g (VCQDs(50 μL)/C3N4) with the increase of the loading amount of VCQDs; and in particular, the surface area of VCQDs(75 μL)/C3N4 is as much as 74 m2/g. The large specific surface area of VCQDs(75 μL)/C3N4 may be attributed to its mesoporous structure with a small pore size. The XRD patterns of VCQDs, C3N4, VCQDs(xμL)/C3N4 series catalysts and CQDs(25 μL)/C3N4 as a reference are shown in Fig. 3 . The broad peak of VCQDs around 2θ = 25.3° corresponds to highly disordered carbon atoms; and according to Scherrer equation, the broadening of this peak is due to the small size effect [29]. All the g–C3N4–containing samples exhibit two characteristic peaks at about 2θ = 12.7° and 27.2°, and they are attributed to the in-plane packing triazine motif of (100) facet with a lattice spacing of 0.67 nm and the graphitic interlayer stacking of aromatic segments of (002) facet of g-C3N4 with a lattice spacing of 0.32 nm, respectively [30]. However, no diffraction signals of CQDs/VCQDs are observed in CQDs(25 μL)/C3N4 and VCQDs(xμL)/C3N4 series catalysts, possibly due to the low amount of CQDs/VCQDs. This indicates that the loaded CQDs/VCQDs do not affect the crystal structure of g-C3N4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analyses of defect state and surface composition
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4 a shows the ESR spectra of CQDs, VCQDs, CQDs(25 μL)/C3N4 and VCQDs(x)/C3N4 measured at room temperature in dark. The Lorentzian line centering at g = 2.005 is assigned to the unpaired electrons in CQDs and VCQDs. VCQDs display a much stronger ESR signal than CQDs, which implies that the former exhibit more unpaired electrons than the latter, due to the presence of carbon vacancy [31]. Besides, the VCQDs(x)/C3N4 series catalysts show stronger ESR signals than CQDs(25 μL)/C3N4, and the intensity of their ESR signals increases with the increase of VCQDs content (Fig. 4b). This further validates the existence of carbon vacancy in the VCQDs(x)/C3N4 series samples. The surface composition and chemical states of g-C3N4, CQDs(25 μL)/C3N4 and VCQDs(25 μL)/C3N4 characterized by XPS are shown in Fig. 5 a. The XPS survey spectra confirm that g-C3N4 and VCQDs(25 μL)/C3N4 are mainly composed of carbon and nitrogen. Aside from C and N elements, CQDs(25 μL)/C3N4 also contains O element possibly coming from the unreacted citric acid. This is consistent with corresponding UV–Vis spectra, which indicates that the addition of urea is beneficial to the conversion of citric acid to carbon quantum dots. Table 2 shows the element composition of g-C3N4, CQDs(25 μL)/C3N4 and VCQDs(25 μL)/C3N4 determined by XPS. The C/N atomic ratio for g-C3N4 is 0.84; and it is 1.48 for CQDs(25 μL)/C3N4 and 0.74 for VCQDs(25 μL)/C3N4, due to the incorporation of CQDs and the introduction of carbon vacancy in VCQDs, respectively. The high-resolution C1s-XPS spectrum of g-C3N4 is shown in Fig. 5b, where the two distinct peaks at 284.8 eV and 288.4 eV are attributed to surface adventitious carbon or graphite sp 2 C–C [32,33] and sp 2-hybridized carbon bonded to N atom within the triazine rings (N–C=N) [32], respectively. Compared to the C1s peak of pure g-C3N4 at 288.4 eV, the C1s peaks of the triazine rings (N–C=N) of both CQDs(25 μL)/C3N4 and VCQDs(25 μL)/C3N4 shift to 288.1 eV and 288.3 eV. This indicates that there is a strong interaction between g-C3N4 matrix and CQDs or VCQDs, which contributes to increasing the electron density of C in C3N4 upon the combination with CQDs/VCQDs.

Fig. 5c displays the FT-IR spectra of g-C3N4 as well as CQDs(25 μL)/C3N4 and VCQDs(25 μL)/C3N4. The absorbance bands in the wavelength range of 1000–1700 cm−1 are attributed to the distinctive stretching vibration of C–N heterocycles, and that at about 808 cm−1 is assigned to the vibration of triazine unit [34,35]. Besides, the vibration bands in the range of 3000–3500 cm−1 might correspond to the surface adsorbed hydroxyl species (C–OH) and free amino groups (N–H) [36]. Moreover, all the three catalysts show similar characteristic peaks of g-C3N4, which indicates that the loading of CQDs/VCQDs does not apparently alter the chemical structure of g-C3N4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical absorbance and band gap structure
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 6 A shows the UV–Vis spectra and photographs of VCQDs (obtained after 5 h reaction) and various CQDs obtained after 5 h, 24 h (denoted as CQDs-24), 48 h (denoted as CQDs-48) and 72 h reaction (denoted as CQDs-72), respectively. VCQDs solution retains brown color even after being diluted by 500-fold, which could be due to the existence of a large amount of carbon vacancy in VCQDs. On the contrary, CQDs solution obtained by heating citric acid solution without urea for the same reaction time of 5 h is almost transparent and colorless (inset in Fig. 6A). When citric acid alone is heated at 180 °C for 24 h, 48 h and 72 h, respectively, the color of the resultant CQDs solution is gradually deepened. This suggests that, during the synthesis of VCQDs, the addition of urea not only can provide gas ammonia reductant to facilitate the generation of carbon vacancy but also can accelerate the formation of carbon quantum dots. Due to the big difference in the visible light absorbance of VCQDs solution and CQDs solution obtained after 5 h of reaction, VCQDs solution was diluted by 500-fold to evaluate the optical properties (UV–Vis spectroscopy), while CQDs solution was undiluted. Both CQDs solution and VCQDs solution show the absorbance peak assigned to the n–π* transition of CO bond around 335 nm, which indicates that they contain carbon quantum dots [37,38]. Besides, the VCQDs solution shows a broad visible absorbance band in the range of 400–470 nm, and this absorbance band is possibly assigned to the defects originated from carbon vacancy. Moreover, under UV irradiation at 365 nm, both CQDs solution and VCQDs solution exhibit bright blue luminescence, the typical character of carbon quantum dots, which further proves that carbon quantum dots are successfully synthesized.

The UV–Vis DRS spectra of g-C3N4, CQDs(25 μL)/C3N4 and VCQDs(xμL)/C3N4 series catalysts are presented in Fig. 6B. Catalyst g-C3N4 shows an absorbance edge around 450 nm. The absorbance edges of CQDs(25 μL)/C3N4 and VCQDs(xμL)/C3N4 are located at 455 nm and 460 nm. Such an apparent red-shift in the absorbance edges of CQDs(25 μL)/C3N4 and VCQDs(xμL)/C3N4 demonstrates that both VCQDs and CQDs can promote the visible light absorbance of g-C3N4, and VCQDs are superior to CQDs in this respect. Moreover, the visible light absorbance of VCQDs(xμL)/C3N4 series catalysts gradually increases with the increase of VCQDs dosage from 10 μL to 75 μL. The inset photograph in Fig. 6B shows that, with the increase of VCQDs content, the color of VCQDs(xμL)/C3N4 catalysts becomes darker gradually, which is consistent with relevant DRS data of the catalysts. From the intercept of (αhυ)1/2 versus hυ curves [39–41] in Fig. 6C, the band gap energies of g-C3N4, CQDs(25 μL)/C3N4 and VCQDs(25 μL)/C3N4 are determined to be 2.67 eV, 2.51 eV and 2.47 eV, respectively. The narrower band gap of VCQDs(25 μL)/C3N4 can promote the generation of more electron-hole pairs and increase the utilization efficiency under solar spectrum.

The Mott-Schottky plot (at frequencies of 1500 Hz and 1800 Hz) and the XPS valence band (VB) spectra of VCQDs(25 μL)/C3N4 are shown in Fig. 7 . The flat band potential (E f) of VCQDs(25 μL)/C3N4 is estimated to be −0.81 eV (vs. Ag/AgCl electrode) [42]. Based on the electric potential of Ag/AgCl electrode (0.2 V), the E f of VCQDs(25 μL)/C3N4 versus a normal hydrogen electrode (NHE) is determined to be −0.61 eV [43,44]. Furthermore, the XPS-VB spectrum (Fig. 7b) demonstrates that the relative potential between the Fermi level and the VB of VCQDs(25 μL)/C3N4 is 1.93 eV. The positive slope in the Mott-Schottky plot of VCQDs(25 μL)/C3N4 suggested that it is an n-type semiconductor, whose Fermi level equals to the flat band potential, −0.61 eV versus NHE [45,46]. Therefore, the VB position of VCQDs(25 μL)/C3N4 is calculated to be 1.32 eV. In combination with the band gap value of 2.47 eV obtained from the DRS spectra, the conduction band potential of VCQDs(25 μL)/C3N4 is determined to be −1.15 eV, more negative than that of E CO2/CO (−0.53 vs. NHE) [47]. This suggests that CO2 could be reduced over VCQDs(25 μL)/C3N4 to generate CO under visible light irradiation.

The photoreduction of CO2 over g-C3N4, CQDs, VCQDs, CQDs(25 μL)/C3N4 and VCQDs(xμL)/C3N4 series catalysts in acetonitrile/H2O solution under visible light irradiation (λ > 400 nm, Beijing Perfect Light, LS-SXE300D, China) was conducted at 25 °C in air. TEOA was added into the reaction system as the sacrificial agent to trap holes, while 20 mg of CoCl2·6H2O and 20 mg of bpy were also added into the reaction system and ultrasonicated for 5 min to form the electron mediator [Co(bpy)3]Cl2. Control experiments show that in the absence of the as-synthesized VCQDs(25 μL)/C3N4 catalyst or CoCl2·6H2O, bpy, TEOA as well as light, no CO and H2 are obtained, which confirms that they play indispensable roles in CO2 photoreduction (Fig. 8 a). When N2 is used to replace CO2, less H2 is produced while no CO is found, which confirms that CO comes from the photocatalytic reduction of CO2.

The main photoreduction products of CO2 over g-C3N4, CQDs(25 μL)/C3N4 and VCQDs(xμL)/C3N4 series catalysts are CO and H2 (Figs. S3a and b). Besides, as shown in Fig. S3c, almost no liquid photoreduction product is detected by high-performance liquid chromatography after illumination for 4 h. Fig. 8b and c shows the dependence of CO and H2 yields on the reaction time. Bare CQDs and VCQDs can be dissolved into the reaction solution and do not show any activity for the photoreduction of CO2. After illumination over pure g-C3N4 for 4 h, CO and H2 are produced at a very small yield of 5 μmol g−1 and 0.94 μmol g−1; and the as-produced syngas has a CO/H2 ratio of 5.32. When g-C3N4 is combined with CQDs or VCQDs, the resultant VCQDs(xμL)/C3N4 series catalysts and CQDs(25 μL)/C3N4 catalyst provide remarkably increased yields of CO and H2 under the same illumination condition; and VCQDs(xμL)/C3N4 series catalysts are highly superior to CQDs(25 μL)/C3N4 and g-C3N4 in terms of the catalytic activity for the photoreduction of CO2 under visible light irradiation. Namely, the yields of CO and H2 after 4 h of illumination over CQDs(25 μL)/C3N4 are 76 μmol g−1 and 680 μmol g−1; and those over VCQDs(25 μL)/C3N4 are as much as 200 μmol g−1 and 1046 μmol g−1 (the as-produced syngas has a H2/CO ratio of 5.23). Such a large enhancement in the photocatalytic activity of VCQDs(25 μL)/C3N4 and CQDs(25 μL)/C3N4 for CO2 photoreduction should be ascribed to the incorporated CQDs or VCQDs. In other words, CQDs or VCQDs incorporated into g-C3N4 matrix are the main active sites for CO2 photoreduction. Besides, as evidenced by corresponding XPS results, the strong interaction between carbon nitride matrix and CQDs or VCQDs also contributes to effectively improving the photocatalytic activity of the heterojunction. Particularly, the carbon vacancy in VCQDs and the strong interfacial interaction between VCQDs and g-C3N4 matrix jointly function to promote electron transport, which is why VCQDs(25 μL)/C3N4 exhibits the highest catalytic activity for CO2 photoreduction. Compared the catalytic activity with other reported photocatalysts toward CO2 photoreduction, VCQDs(25 μL)/C3N4 catalyst also showed obvious good activity (Table S1). During the reaction process, the photogenerated h+ would react with TEOA or H2O to produce 2-[bis(2-methoxyethyl)amino]acetaldehyde verified by GC-MS and O2 (Figs. S3 and S5). Only when the photogenerated holes are consumed in the oxidation of TEOA and H2O, the photogenerated electrons can be better separated to reduce CO2 and H+.

The turnover number (TON) and the turnover frequency values (TOF) of various catalysts are shown in Table S2. The TOF value for CO production during 4 h of illumination over VCQDs(25 μL)/C3N4 is 425.0 h−1, much larger than that over CQDs(25 μL)/C3N4 under the same illumination condition (111.5 h−1), which further demonstrates that VCQDs as the photocatalytic active sites for CO2 photoreduction are more efficient than CQDs. More important, the H2:CO ratio of the as-produced syngas within 4 h reaction gradually increases with the increase in the loading amount of VCQDs (Fig. 8e), which indicates that the proportion of the syngas could be tuned by manipulating the dosage of VCQDs in the heterojunction catalyst. In addition, the ratio of H2: CO in produced syngas during different reaction time over various catalysts are shown in Fig. 4S. It can be seen that, the ratio of H2: CO largely decrease with the increase of reaction time, which indicates that the hydrogen is easily formed at the beginning of the reaction, while the generation of CO may require multiple reaction steps. Certainly, Fig. 4S also shows that after 3 h of reaction, the ratio of H2: CO is almost unchanged. So, the desired H2: CO ratio can be obtained after a period of reaction over certain catalyst.

In order to explore the effect of the adding mode of [Co(bpy)3]Cl2, the electron mediator, on the catalytic activity of the heterojunction catalysts for CO2 photoreduction, we compared the generation rates of CO and H2 over VCQDs(25 μL)/C3N4 catalyst upon the addition of [Co(bpy)3]Cl2 in the reaction system in three modes (see relevant experimental section). As shown in Fig. 9 a, the H2/CO ratio for the syngas produced over VCQDs(25 μL)/C3N4 catalyst upon the introduction of [Co(bpy)3]Cl2 in modes A and C is 3; and the generation rates of CO and H2 under mode C is higher than those under mode A. This means that mode C is superior to mode A in terms of the CO and H2 generations via CO2 photoreduction has a H2/CO ratio more suitable for industrial application. Therefore, the as-synthesized photosensitizer, [Co(bpy)3]Cl2·6H2O, is suggested to be directly added into the reaction system (mode C) for the photocatalytic reduction of CO2, thereby affording syngas with a desired H2/CO ratio.

Aside from [Co(bpy)3]Cl2·6H2O, the electron mediators [Ni(bpy)3]Cl2·6H2O and [Ru(bpy)3]Cl2·6H2O were also added into the reaction system and comparatively investigated (Fig. 9b, c, d and e). Corresponding UV–Vis spectra and FT-IR spectra (Figs. S6a and b) can prove the successful synthesis of [Co(bpy)3]Cl2·6H2O and [Ni(bpy)3]Cl2·6H2O electron mediators. The [Ru(bpy)3]Cl2 electron mediator was purchased from Bide Pharmmatech Company Limited (Shanghai, China), due to the difficult synthesis. As can be seen in Fig. 9e, the best selectivity is obtained in the presence of [Co(bpy)3]Cl2; and in this case only CO and H2 are produced at high generation rates of 56 μmol h−1 · g−1 and 170 μmol h−1 · g−1 while the H2: CO ratio is about 3 : 1. In the presence of [Ni(bpy)3]Cl2·6H2O and [Ru(bpy)3]Cl2·6H2O, CH4 is also produced to accompany CO and H2. Therefore, [Co(bpy)3]Cl2·6H2O is suggested to be the best electron mediator in the present research.

The photocatalytic activity of VCQDs(25 μL)/C3N4 catalyst possessing wide scope visible light absorbance is further examined under irradiation from 420 nm to 520 nm (Fig. 10 ); and the apparent quantum yields (AQY) at corresponding wavelengths are also calculated (Table S3). It can be seen that, although the apparent quantum yields of CO and H2 decrease gradually with the decrease of irradiation energy, the AQYCO and AQYH2 under the irradiation at 520 nm are still up to 0.02% and 0.31%. This indicates that the VCQDs(25 μL)/C3N4 heterojunction is applicable to CO2 photoreduction under visible light irradiation yielding syngas.

In industrial production, how to control the ratio of H2: CO in syngas is a great challenge. According to above results, it can be seen that the type and the adding mode of electron mediator, irradiation energy and the loading amount of VCQDs all can affect the composition of syngas. However, only the content of VCQDs can affect the proportion of H2 and CO regularly. So, the ratio of H2: CO in syngas can be controlled by adjusting the amount of loaded VCQDs on the surface of g-C3N4 matrix.

The stability of VCQDs(25 μL)/C3N4 photocatalyst examined by recycling experiment under visible light irradiation is presented in Fig. 11 a, where the catalyst, sacrificial agent and electron mediator are not replenished throughout the cycling processes, only new high-purity CO2 gas was introduced to replace the gas of the previous cycle. After 4 run of recycle experiments, the catalytic activity of VCQDs(25 μL)/C3N4 for the photoreduction of CO2 generating syngas slightly decreases, which demonstrates that the as-synthesized photocatalyst exhibits good stability. The comparison of the FT-IR spectra and XRD patterns of VCQDs(25 μL)/C3N4 before and after 4 run of recycle experiments further confirms that the tested catalyst exhibits good stability (Fig. 11b and c), which could be attributed to the strong interfacial interaction between VCQDs and g-C3N4.

The photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra of g-C3N4, CQDs(25 μL)/C3N4 and VCQDs(25 μL)/C3N4 under the excited wavelength of 325 nm were measured to investigate the separation efficiency of photoexcited e−-h+ pairs and explore why CQDs(25 μL)/C3N4 heterojunction and VCQDs(25 μL)/C3N4 heterojunction exhibit enhanced catalytic activity for the photoreduction of CO2. Fig. 12 a displays the PL emission spectra of g-C3N4, CQDs(25 μL)/C3N4 and VCQDs(25 μL)/C3N4. The intensity of the PL spectra of both CQDs(25 μL)/C3N4 and VCQDs(25 μL)/C3N4 is considerably lower than that of pure g-C3N4, and the peak of VCQDs(25 μL)/C3N4 is weaker than the one of CQDs(25 μL)/C3N4. This indicates that VCQDs(25 μL)/C3N4 and CQDs(25 μL)/C3N4 upon irradiation at 325 nm can yield photogenerated carriers with much higher separation efficiencies than those of pure g-C3N4, due to the strong electron-capturing ability of CQDs/VCQDs. Besides, the separation efficiency of the e−-h+ pairs of VCQDs(25 μL)/C3N4 is higher than that of CQDs(25 μL)/C3N4, possibly because the carbon vacancy in VCQDs can enhance the electron transport ability. To obtain more details about the decay behavior of the photogenerated carriers of the tested catalysts, we measured their time-resolved fluorescence spectra. As shown in Fig. 12b and Table S4, the PL signals of CQDs(25 μL)/C3N4 and VCQDs(25 μL)/C3N4 tend to decay faster than that of pristine g-C3N4. According to the fitting results, the average PL lifetime (τ a) of CQDs(25 μL)/C3N4 (7.82 ns) and VCQDs(25 μL)/C3N4 (7.44 ns) is shorter than that of pristine g-C3N4 (11.27 ns), and the τ a of VCQDs(25 μL)/C3N4 is the shortest. Specifically, the contribution of τ 1 (fast decay component, related to electron transfer) increases largely from 49.08% for g-C3N4 to 59.14% for CQDs(25 μL)/C3N4 and 55.91% for VCQDs(25 μL)/C3N4. This further confirms that the introduction of CQDs/VCQDS can accelerate the photogenerated electron transfer from g-C3N4 to CQDs/VCQDS, thereby largely inhibiting the radiative recombination [48–50]. For further clarifying the high activity of the tested photocatalysts towards CO2 photoreduction, their transient photocurrent responses and electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) are measured in relation to the photogenerated charge transfer and separation abilities. The transient photocurrent response i–t curves clearly reveal that CQDs(25 μL)/C3N4 and VCQDs(25 μL)/C3N4 exhibit a higher photocurrent density than g-C3N4 (Fig. 12c). Especially, the photocurrent density of VCQDs(25 μL)/C3N4 (about 0.70 μA/cm2), is approximately 3.5 times as much as that of pristine g-C3N4 (about 0.20 μA/cm2), which implies that the introduction of VCQDs contributes to greatly promoting the photoexcited charge carrier transfer in VCQDs(25 μL)/C3N4. Corresponding EIS data demonstrate that VCQDs(25 μL)/C3N4 and CQDs(25 μL)/C3N4 have a smaller charge transfer resistance than g-C3N4 (see Fig. 11d). Particularly, the charge transfer resistance of VCQDs(25 μL)/C3N4 is smaller than that of CQDs(25 μL)/C3N4, which well corresponds to the better photoinduced electron transmission ability and photocatalytic activity of the former. In summary, VCQDs/CQDs can significantly enhance the electronic conductivity, and the carbon vacancy incorporated into carbon quantum dots contributes to further improving the electron transport ability of the VCQDs(x)/C3N4 heterojunction.

To further explore the promotion effect of [Co(bpy)3]Cl2 on the electron transfer during the photocatalytic reduction of CO2, the comparison of the transient photocurrent with and without [Co(bpy)3]Cl2 has been conducted. A large increase in the transient photocurrent in the presence of [Co(bpy)3]Cl2 (Fig. 13 ) suggests that the electron mediator, [Co(bpy)3]Cl2, can solve the bottleneck hindering the charge transfer. The CV of solution in the presence of various amount [Co(bpy)3]Cl2 under different atmosphere has been operated. As shown in Fig. 14 , under both Ar and CO2 atmosphere, the CV curves of solution appear linear in the absence of [Co(bpy)3]Cl2. After addition of [Co(bpy)3]Cl2, the obvious current enhancement in CV can be seen, and the current gradually increased with the increase of [Co(bpy)3]Cl2 added. This suggest that [Co(bpy)3]Cl2 can promote the charge carriers transfer and separation during the catalytic process of CO2 reduction. Moreover, the CV curves of solution in the presence of [Co(bpy)3]Cl2 appear two irreversible peaks at −1.0 V and −1.4 V, which maybe correspond to [Co(bpy)3]2+/[Co(bpy)3]+ and successive [Co(bpy)3CO2]+ adduct, respectively [51]. This assumption can be verified by the UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy of the solution after 4 h irradiation. As shown in Fig. 15 , after irradiation, a new broad absorption band (420–600 nm) appears, accompanied by the red shift of the initial absorption band at ca. 300 nm. The color of the reaction mixture also changed from buff to light red upon irradiation for 4 h (inset in Fig. 15). The broad absorption band is attributed to the intramolecular charge-transfer from metal to pyridinium moiety.

Based on the above experimental results, the possible mechanism of [Co(bpy)3]Cl2 mediated CO2 and H2O reduction over VCQDs(25 μL)/C3N4 or CQDs(25 μL)/C3N4 under visible light irradiation generating CO and H2 is proposed in Fig. 16 . Upon visible light irradiation of VCQDs(25 μL)/C3N4 or CQDs(25 μL)/C3N4, the electrons in the valence band of g-C3N4 are photoexcited to the conduction band, leaving holes in the VB of g-C3N4. Having strong electronic conductivity, VCQDs or CQDs could act as the bridge to transfer the excited CB electron to [Co(bpy)3]2+ mediator. The photogenerated holes in the VB of g-C3N4 can react with TEOA, thereby being consumed. As a result, the electrons reduce adsorbed [Co(bpy)3]2+ into [Co(bpy)3]+, and the [Co(bpy)3]+ has a strong interaction with CO2 (carbon atom is electron deficiency) to form a metal carbonate [51,52]. The generation of [Co(bpy)3]+ has been convinced by CV curves (Fig. 14) and UV–Vis spectra (Fig. 15). Then, the protonation occurs, providing oxygen atom from activated CO2 molecules to react with H+. Meanwhile, the reduction product CO can yield. The carbon vacancy in VCQDs can enhance the electron transport ability and improve the separation efficiency of the photogenerated carriers, thereby increasing the photocatalytic activity of VCQDs(25 μL)/C3N4 for the photoreduction of CO2. On the other hand, during the CO2 reduction process, the competitive reaction of hydrogen generation also takes place on the surface of VCQDs or CQDs for the strong electronic conductivity [53]. The more the loaded VCQDs, the more H+ was reduced into hydrogen gas, and the less protonation of metal carbonate occurred to generate CO. So, the ratio of H2:CO in syngas product can be controlled by the amount of loaded VCQDs. In addition, from Fig. 16, it can be seen that, the conversion of CO2 into CO is more complex than the generation of hydrogen and requires several steps, which can be confirmed by the changing trend of H2/CO ratio during the reaction process over various catalysts (Fig. 4S).

A series of VCQDs(xμL)/C3N4 heterostructured photocatalysts with carbon vacancy in VCQDs is successfully synthesized via a facile hydrothermal method. During the hydrothermal process, a large amount of ammonia gas is released from the added urea to etch the structure of the carbon quantum dots, yielding carbon vacancy in VCQDs. The carbon vacancies can reduce the band gap of VCQDs(xμL)/C3N4, thereby improving its visible light absorbance ability; and in the meantime, they can enhance the electron transport ability of the heterostructured photocatalyst, thereby facilitating the charge carrier separation. This, in combination with the strong interfacial interaction between VCQDs and g-C3N4, accounts for the better catalytic activity of VCQDs(25 μL)/C3N4 than that of CQDs(25 μL)/C3N4 for the photoreduction of CO2 under visible light irradiation. More important, various factors can affect the composition of syngas, and the H2: CO ratio of the as-produced syngas over VCQDs(25 μL)/C3N4 can be easilly tuned by altering the loading amount of VCQDs, which is of particular significance for the industrial application of the syngas produced via visible light catalytic reduction of CO2 and for relieving the adverse climate effects brought by CO2 emission.

Ruifeng Chong: and gave some suggestion about the photocatalytic mechanism. Xiaoqiang Liu: gave some suggestion about the photocatalytic mechanism. All authors read and contributed to the manuscript.
